summary: Before I enlisted, I was working a civil service job at the Brooklyn Navy Yard in New York. I saw how the sailors there always got their food and worked on clean ships, which inspired me to join the Navy and Coast Guard instead of the Army. It was around 1942 when I was living in Brooklyn and decided to enlist.

I served in the United States Coast Guard during World War II, enlisting in July 1942. My role was a Storekeeper, where I handled supplies and payroll on the ship. Later, I was made a chief storekeeper and transferred to Coast Guard headquarters in Washington, D.C., where I met my wife Joan, who worked in cryptography.

I served four years in World War II, from 1942 until 1946, even after the war ended in 1945. I was part of many operations, including battles near New Guinea, the Philippines, and the Aleutian Islands. The war shaped much of my life, and I witnessed combat like the battle at Leyte Gulf, though I was mostly optimistic throughout.

After the war ended, I was stationed near Attu Island in the Aleutians. It was there that we turned our ship, the USS Bisbee, over to the Russians under the lend-lease plan. This happened around 1945, marking the end of my active service before I was discharged in 1946.

During my service, I was part of the lend-lease plan where the United States gave ships and material to the Russians. For example, we turned over our ship, the USS Bisbee, to the Russians in 1945 near Attu Island. This was part of a larger agreement to support the Soviet Union during the war.

I was awarded a medal for service in the American Theater during World War II. This medal was common for those who served in operations around the US Atlantic or Pacific areas. It recognized the work we did close to home as well as overseas.

I also received a medal for the Asiatic-Pacific campaign, which covered my service in places like New Guinea and the Philippines. We spent many months in this theater, participating in battles and supporting operations in the Pacific. It was a significant part of my wartime experience.

I was proud to receive the Good Conduct Medal during my time in the Coast Guard. It meant I stayed out of trouble and did my duties well throughout my four years of service. They called me a 'good boy' in the service, and the medal was a nice recognition of that.

My job in the Coast Guard was as a Storekeeper, where I was responsible for managing all the supplies and handling payroll on the ship. I was known for always getting the payroll ready on time, which made me popular among the crew. Later, I was promoted to chief storekeeper and transferred to headquarters.

One of my most memorable experiences was at Leyte Gulf in the Philippines during World War II. We were about 10 miles from the massive naval battle, watching shells fly between the American and Japanese ships. It was like watching a movie, with hundreds of planes involved, and luckily, we were not directly attacked.

I served aboard the USS Bisbee, a patrol frigate designated PF-46, during World War II. The ship saw action in the Pacific, including near New Guinea and Leyte Gulf. After the war, we turned the Bisbee over to the Russians as part of lend-lease, and it was later returned to Yokosuka, Japan, around 1950 during the Korean War.

After we handed over the USS Bisbee to the Russians, we went to Seattle, Washington, where I stayed for a week or two. It was there I was promoted to chief storekeeper and then transferred to Coast Guard headquarters. Seattle was a brief but important stop before moving on to Washington, D.C.

My wife Joan worked in cryptography during the war, stationed at a naval office connected to the Coast Guard. She operated IBM machines that sent out codes to ships at sea. I met her in early 1945 when I was transferred to Coast Guard headquarters in Washington, D.C.

Joan, my wife, worked with IBM machines during World War II as part of her cryptography duties. These machines were used to send coded messages to ships at sea, which was critical for naval communication. This was at the naval office in Washington, D.C., where I met her.

In early 1945, I was transferred to Coast Guard headquarters in Washington, D.C., where I met my future wife Joan. She was working in cryptography there, and I stayed in D.C. until my discharge in June 1946. It was an important place for the final part of my service.

After the war, the USS Bisbee was returned by Russia in Yokosuka, Japan, around 1950 during the Korean War. This was notable because we had originally given the ship to the Russians in 1945. Yokosuka was a key naval base where the ship ended up after its service with the Soviet Navy.

The Gallup, designated PF-47, was a sister ship to the USS Bisbee, PF-46. Both ships were given to the Russians under lend-lease in 1945. I saw a newspaper article mentioning the Gallup being returned by Russia in 1950, similar to the Bisbee's fate during the Korean War period.

Although I did not serve in the Korean War, I learned that the USS Bisbee, which I served on during World War II, was returned by Russia in Yokosuka, Japan, in 1950, during the Korean War. This showed me how the ship continued to have a role even after my service ended.

My combat experience began around New Guinea, a large island north of Australia, where we operated for about seven months. We were involved in supporting operations on New Guinea and nearby islands before moving on to the Philippines. It was an intense period early in my service during World War II.

During the war, we operated near Australia, especially around New Guinea and the islands leading up to the Philippines. Australian soldiers sometimes came aboard our ship after missions, bringing back evidence of their combat. Australia was part of the broader Pacific theater where I served.

I was not aware of the Holocaust while I was serving in the war; I only learned about its full horror after I returned home. We didn't have the kind of news coverage or television like today, so information was limited. When I found out, I was, of course, horrified like everyone else.

The attack on Pearl Harbor happened in December 1941, about seven months before I enlisted in the service. I remember the date well, but I joined the Coast Guard in mid-1942, so I was not yet in the military when it occurred. The attack was a pivotal moment that led many, including myself, to enlist.
